sections.content:

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 2 has 5 green crosses. Top panel: 4 crosses at row 16.5, cols 14.5, 20.5, 32.5, 44.5. Bottom panel: 1 cross at (52.5, 44.5). Yellow/orange cell at (34.5, 32.5) - possibly a target.
- Goal model: Reduce to 1 cross using jumps.
- Action model: Same as Level 1 - click cross → direction → click target.
- Plan: First, try jumping the leftmost cross RIGHT over the adjacent cross.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very revealing! Looking at the transitions:
- Transition -2 (MOUSE at 26): Before and After are the SAME - 3 crosses at 38.5, 44.5, 44.5. The board didn't change!
- Last transition (MOUSE at 32): Before and After are the SAME - 3 crosses at 38.5, 44.5, 44.5. The board didn't change!
This means the reset (MOUSE at 58, 6) and the subsequent actions (MOUSE at 14, MOUSE at 26, MOUSE at 32) didn't actually change the board state! The board was ALREADY at crosses 38.5, 44.5 before these actions.
Wait, but the history shows the reset action. Let me check: maybe the reset action (entry 6) was the last action that actually changed the board, and entries 7-9 were selection-only actions that didn't change the board.
Actually, looking more carefully at the transitions output:
- Transition -2 is MOUSE(row=16, col=26) - this is the jump action
- Before: 3 crosses at 38.5, 44.5, 44.5
- After: 3 crosses at 38.5, 44.5, 44.5 (SAME!)
This means the jump at (16, 26) didn't do anything! The board was already at 38.5, 44.5. This suggests the reset didn't work, or the board was already in this state.
Wait, I think I understand now. The transitions list only contains actions that actually changed the board. So:
- Transition -2 (MOUSE at 26) had no board change, meaning it was just a selection
- Last transition (MOUSE at 32) also had no board change
This means the last board-changing action was the reset (entry 6), which somehow resulted in the board being at 38.5, 44.5. But that doesn't make sense for a reset!
Unless... the "reset" button at (58, 6) doesn't actually reset the board. Maybe it does something else. Let me check what happens when I click it.
Actually, I think the issue is that the board was ALREADY in the state with crosses at 38.5, 44.5 from a previous failed attempt. The "reset" button might not be working as I expect, or the game auto-reset to a different state.
Let me just accept the current state and work from here. I have crosses at 38.5 and 44.5 in the top panel. From my earlier experiments:
- Jumping 44.5 LEFT over 38.5 results in 0 crosses (failure)
- Cross at 38.5 can't jump RIGHT
So I'm stuck with 2 crosses. Let me try clicking the yellow/orange target at (34.5, 32.5) to see if that completes the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# KEY INSIGHT: The transitions show NO board changes for the last 2 actions!
# The board was already at crosses 38.5, 44.5 before these actions.
# This means the "reset" at (58,6) didn't reset to initial state - it went to 38.5, 44.5.
#
# Current state: 2 top crosses at 38.5, 44.5 + 1 bottom cross at 44.5
# Only valid jump: 44.5 LEFT over 38.5 → 0 crosses (failure)
#
# NEW PLAN: Try clicking the yellow/orange target at (34.5, 32.5) to complete the level.

predict(expect_change=True, note='Click yellow target to complete level')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 32}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green crosses after: {len(green)}")
for n in green:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  {center}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  (16.5, 38.5)
  (16.5, 44.5)
  (52.5, 44.5)
Level completed: False
Done: False
